metadata.notes: Standalone sequel to Above and Below. Reimplements base game with ghosts, boats, pearls, dangerous caves, adventure cards, and banish action. Source: official Red Raven Games Dropbox PDF.

subtopics.title: Components
subtopics.content:
#### Boards & Books
- 4 Player Boards (ready area, exhausted area, injured area, advancement track)
- 1 Reputation Board (villager market, reputation track, time track, ingredient track)
- 1 Encounter Book (numbered paragraphs with branching narrative encounters)
- 1 Round Marker, 1 First-Player Card

#### Cards
- 23 House Cards (buildings for your village surface)
- 22 Outpost Cards (underground buildings placed on cave cards)
- 8 Star House Cards (premium buildings, always available)
- 9 Key House Cards (5 used per game, powerful effects)
- 4 Starting House Cards (1 per player)
- 25 Cave Cards (15 basic, 10 dangerous with red serpent icon)
- 43 Adventure Cards (unique rewards gained from encounters)

#### Villagers
- 12 Starting Villagers (3 per player: 1 hammer, 1 quill, 1 explorer)
- 22 Regular Villagers (recruited during play)
- 2 Special Villagers (Cave Cat and Grey Cat — gained from encounters)

#### Tokens
- 81 Goods Tokens: Common (rice, clams, crabs), Uncommon (tea leaves, nuts, thornfruit), Rare (squidroot, rubyfish)
- Coins (36 ones, 9 fives, 6 tens)
- 18 Mystic Brew Tokens (refresh injured or exhausted villagers)
- 15 Boat Tokens (change a die to 6 or gain 1 coin)
- 14 Pearl Tokens (earned from dangerous cave successes, count as your highest good)
- 24 Ghost Tokens (haunt your buildings, reduce reputation)
- 36 Wooden Cubes (9 per player color)
- 7 Dice

subtopics.title: Game Structure
subtopics.content:
#### Overview
Above and Below: Haunted is played over exactly 7 rounds. Each round, players take turns clockwise assigning villagers to perform actions. When a player has no more ready villagers or chooses not to act, they pass. Once all players pass, the round ends and cleanup steps occur.

#### Turn Flow
On your turn, choose one action: Explore, Build, Banish, Hire, or Labor. Each action requires exhausting one or more villagers (moving them from ready to exhausted or injured). You may also perform any number of free actions before or after your main action.

#### The Three Areas
Your player board has three villager zones:
- **Ready Area** — Villagers available to assign this round
- **Exhausted Area** — Used villagers resting until refreshed
- **Injured Area** — Villagers who were exerted during exploration; need mystic brew or extra beds to recover

#### Building Your Village
You build above ground (houses in a row) and below ground (outposts placed on cave cards). Surface buildings are easier to get — underground outposts require exploring first to gain cave cards, but often score more.

#### The Encounter Book
When you explore, another player reads you a narrative passage from the encounter book. You choose your response, roll dice, and the outcome determines your rewards. This storytelling element is the heart of the game.

#### Winning
After 7 rounds, count village points from your advancement track, buildings, reputation, card bonuses, and ingredient track. Most points wins.

subtopics.title: Detailed Rules
subtopics.content:
#### Player Actions

**Explore** — Draw a cave card (basic or dangerous) and place it beside your board. Send 2 or more villagers onto it. Roll 1 die per villager. The player to your left reads the encounter paragraph and choices aloud (but NOT the rewards in parentheses). Choose a response and try to meet or exceed its explore number with your total lanterns.

Die results: Roll 4-6 = 2 lanterns, Roll 2-3 = 1 lantern, Roll 1 = 0 lanterns. Villagers can be exerted (moved to injured) for +1 lantern each. Exceed the explore number by 2+ to earn bonus rewards. You keep the cave card even if you fail. Successful dangerous cave exploration also earns 1 pearl.

**Build** — Exhaust 1 hammer villager. Purchase any available house, star house, key house, or outpost card by paying its coin cost. Outposts must be placed on an available (empty) cave card. Houses go in a row beside your starting house. Draw a replacement card for the purchased slot.

**Banish** — Exhaust 1 quill villager. Requires at least 1 ghost on your buildings. Return all your ghosts to the supply except 1. Place that ghost on an opponent's building that doesn't already have one. Gain a reward based on the building type: no-bed building = 2 reputation + 2 coins; bed building = 1 mystic brew + 3 coins; outpost = 1 mystic brew + 1 boat.

**Hire** — Exhaust 1 quill villager. Pay the coin cost of a villager on the reputation board. Place them in your exhausted area. Hiring from slots 1-3 (cheapest) = gain 1 ghost on a building. Hiring from slots 4-5 = gain 1 mystic brew instead. Do not replace the villager until end of round.

**Labor** — Exhaust 1 or more villagers. For each villager: gain 1 coin AND you may harvest 1 good from one of your houses or outposts. Hammer villagers harvest 2 goods instead of 1. Harvested goods can be kept, placed for sale, or added to your advancement track.

#### Free Actions (no villager cost)
- **Buy from a player** — Purchase a good/mystic brew marked for sale (minimum 3 coins/mystic brews)
- **Put something for sale** — Place a good or mystic brew in your sale area
- **Refresh building row** — Pay 1 coin to replace all 4 house or outpost cards (once per turn)
- **Claim an ingredient** — First player to harvest/gain a good type places their cube on the ingredient track
- **Use a boat** — Move a ready boat to exhausted: gain 1 coin OR change any die to a 6 during exploration

#### Advancement Track
Goods placed left-to-right on your player board's bottom row. Same good type stacks on one slot. Left number above slot = VP per good at game end. Right number = your current income level. Once placed, goods cannot be removed.

#### Beds & Refreshing
At end of round, for each bed symbol on your buildings, move 1 villager one zone: injured→exhausted OR exhausted→ready. Mystic brews can also move 1 villager one zone at this time. Boats return to ready automatically.

subtopics.title: Ghosts & New Mechanics
subtopics.content:
#### Ghosts
Ghosts haunt your buildings. Each building can hold 1 ghost. You gain ghosts primarily from hiring villagers through the cheaper marsh route (slots 1-3). At the end of each round, you lose 1 reputation for each ghost on your buildings. Use the Banish action to clear ghosts and send one to an opponent.

#### Dangerous Caves
The cave card deck includes 10 dangerous caves (marked with a red serpent and pearl icon). These have higher explore numbers (5-11 vs basic 2-6) but reward a pearl token on success. Dangerous caves offer greater risk and reward.

#### Pearls
Pearl tokens are gained from successful dangerous cave explorations. At game end, each pearl counts as your highest-valued good type in your advancement track for scoring purposes.

#### Boats
Boat tokens go in your ready area. As a free action, move a ready boat to exhausted to either gain 1 coin or change any die roll to a 6 during exploration. Boats return to ready during end-of-round refresh. Some buildings and encounter rewards grant boats.

#### Adventure Cards
The 43 adventure cards are gained as encounter rewards. Each has a unique ability described on the card. Keep them in numerical order and do not read faces until drawn.

#### Mystic Brew
Mystic brew tokens replace cider and potions from the original game. Each mystic brew moves 1 villager one zone (injured→exhausted or exhausted→ready) during the rest step. Can also be used as payment (minimum 3 coins/mystic brews) when buying from other players.

#### Ingredient Track
On the reputation board. When you are the first player to harvest or gain a particular goods type, place your cube on that good's space. At game end: common goods (rice, clam, crab) = 1 VP each; uncommon (tea leaf, nut, thornfruit) = 2 VP each; rare (squidroot, rubyfish) = 4 VP each.

#### Special Villagers
- **Cave Cat / Grey Cat** — Gained from encounters. Roll a die each time you assign them. Roll 1-2: cat refuses and goes to exhausted without completing the action. Roll 3-6: works normally.
- **Ancient Robot** — Cannot use building beds or mystic brews. Has its own built-in bed.

subtopics.title: End of Game & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### Game End
The game ends after 7 rounds (when the round marker cannot advance further).

#### Scoring Categories

1. **Advancement Track** — Each good scores VP based on its slot position. The number above the slot indicates VP per good in that pile.

2. **Buildings** — 1 VP per building you own (houses, key houses, star houses, outposts, and your starting house). Empty cave cards do NOT count.

3. **Reputation** — The player with the most reputation gains 5 VP, second-most gains 3 VP, third-most gains 2 VP. In a 2-player game, only the leader gains 3 VP. Ties: add the tied positions' VP together and divide by 2, rounding up. Additionally, gain or lose VP based on your position on the reputation track.

4. **Card Bonuses** — Many buildings grant flat VP or conditional VP (e.g., +2 VP per rubyfish owned, +2 VP per house and boat).

5. **Ingredient Track** — VP for each of your cubes on the ingredient track (1/2/4 VP by rarity).

6. **Pearls** — Each pearl counts as your highest-valued good type for scoring.

#### Tiebreakers (in order)
1. Most coins
2. Most villagers and boats
3. Most buildings

subtopics.title: Beginner Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### The 7-Round Clock
You only have 7 rounds. This is not a long game — every action matters. Plan your first few rounds carefully: what will you build, when will you explore, and how will you grow your workforce?

#### Explore vs Build Timing
Exploring early is valuable because it gives you cave cards for future outpost construction and encounter rewards that snowball. But building early gives you beds (critical for refreshing workers) and income bonuses. A strong opening is: explore in rounds 1-2 to get cave cards and rewards, then build outposts on those cards in rounds 3-4.

#### Worker Recruitment Tempo
More villagers means more actions per round. Hiring aggressively in the first 2-3 rounds pays dividends because each new villager gives you one extra action every remaining round. The ghost from cheap hires is manageable if you plan to banish later. Hiring from slots 4-5 costs more but gives mystic brew instead of a ghost.

#### Reading Encounter Choices
When the encounter is read, pay attention to the explore numbers. Lower explore numbers are safer but often give lesser rewards. If you have 3+ villagers with good dice values, aim for the harder choice with better rewards. Remember: exerting villagers (moving them to injured) gives +1 lantern each, and boats can change a die to 6. Factor these into your decision.

#### Ghost Management
Ghosts cost you 1 reputation each per round. A ghost or two early is fine, but letting them accumulate means losing 3-4 reputation per round, which adds up fast. Time your banish actions strategically — banishing clears ALL your ghosts and lets you send one to an opponent.

#### Advancement Track and Income
Place diverse goods to increase your income. Your starting income is only 3 coins, but filling advancement slots quickly gets it to 5-7 coins per round. Prioritize getting different goods types rather than stacking one type. Also try to claim ingredient track spots for rare goods (squidroot, rubyfish = 4 VP each).

#### Dangerous Caves and Pearls
Dangerous caves have explore numbers of 5-11. Send your best explorers and consider using boat tokens to guarantee a 6. Each pearl counts as your highest good at game end, which can be worth 3+ VP each. Worth the risk if you have strong villagers.

#### Key Buildings to Watch For
- Buildings with beds are essential — they refresh your workers
- Income buildings compound every round
- Star houses are always available and offer strong VP
- Key houses often have powerful ongoing effects
- Outposts require cave cards but frequently score more than surface buildings
- Buildings that hire to ready (instead of exhausted) are extremely powerful

#### Common First-Game Mistakes
- Not recruiting enough villagers early — you need workers to take actions
- Ignoring beds — without them, your exhausted villagers stay stuck
- Hoarding goods instead of placing them on the advancement track for income
- Forgetting the game is only 7 rounds — there's no time for a slow start
- Letting ghosts pile up without banishing — reputation loss compounds

rulings.ruling: You must send at least 2 villagers when exploring. There is no upper limit to how many you can send.
You keep the cave card even if you fail the encounter. This is a change from the original Above and Below.
Each building can only hold one ghost. If you gain a ghost but have no buildings without ghosts, you gain no ghost.
The reader reads the full paragraph including choices and explore numbers, but does NOT read the rewards listed in parentheses after each explore number.
After rolling, you may exert any villager (move them to injured instead of exhausted) to gain +1 lantern. This is optional and done after seeing your die results.
You gain bonus rewards (in addition to basic rewards) only if you exceed the required explore number by 2 or more lanterns.
Free actions can be performed before or after your main action on your turn. Once you declare 'I pass,' you may not take any more actions or free actions that round.
A boat can either gain 1 coin OR change a die to 6 during exploration, not both. You may use a boat after rolling the die. Boats return to ready during end-of-round refresh without needing beds.
Hiring from slots 1-3 (cheapest) gives you a ghost on one of your buildings. Hiring from slots 4-5 gives you a mystic brew instead. The villager goes to your exhausted area either way.
Pearl tokens are earned only from successful dangerous cave explorations. At game end, each pearl counts as your highest-valued good type in your advancement track.
Goods must be placed left to right with no gaps. Same good types must stack on the same slot. Once placed, goods cannot be removed. The rightmost occupied slot determines your income level.
In a 4-player game, each player places only the hammer villager in the ready area. The other two starting villagers begin in the exhausted area and become ready after the first round.
